title: Uber — Ride Matching System Design
description: Designing the core of a ride-hailing platform: match a rider requesting a trip with a nearby available driver, in seconds, while millions of drivers stream their location continuously. The hard problems are geospatial — indexing moving points and querying 'who is near me' at scale — plus matching under contention, real-time location ingest, and surge pricing. We build from a naive nearest-driver scan toward a geo-indexed, region-sharded matching system.

nodes.label: Ride API
nodes.description: Ride API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Ride API Gateway
nodes.description: Ride API Gateway: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Dispatch
nodes.description: Dispatch: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Trip 1
nodes.description: Trip 1: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.description: Trip 2: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Dispatch Service
nodes.description: Dispatch Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Driver App
nodes.description: Driver App: Software client outside the backend boundary that streams location and receives trip offers.

nodes.label: ETA / Routing Service
nodes.description: ETA / Routing Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.description: Geo Index: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.description: Geo Index — Region A: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.description: Geo Index — driver cells: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Idempotency/Lock
nodes.description: Idempotency/Lock: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Idempotency Store
nodes.description: Idempotency Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Lock/CAS
nodes.description: Lock/CAS: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.description: Location Ingest Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.description: Driver Location Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.description: Location Stream: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Road Graph / Map Data
nodes.description: Road Graph / Map Data: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.description: Matching Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.description: Push Notifications: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.description: Observability: Metrics, logs, and traces — location-stream lag, geo-query latency, offer timeout/acceptance rate, CAS conflict rate, hot-cell cardinality, and per-region error-budget burn.

nodes.label: Location Auth / Risk
nodes.description: Location Auth / Risk: Authenticates and validates driver pings (device identity, ping timestamp, monotonic sequence) and runs anti-spoofing checks before a location reaches the index.

nodes.label: Pricing Service
nodes.description: Pricing Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Rider
nodes.description: Rider: Human or organizational role that initiates, receives, approves, or operates interactions with the system.

nodes.label: Matching — Region A
nodes.description: Matching — Region A: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Matching — Region B
nodes.description: Matching — Region B: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Rider App
nodes.description: Rider App: Software client outside the backend boundary that requests trips and shows live ETA.

nodes.label: Region Router
nodes.description: Region Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Surge / Demand Store
nodes.description: Surge / Demand Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.description: Trip Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Trips DB
nodes.description: Trips DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

functional: A rider requests a ride from a pickup location to a destination.
Drivers continuously report location and availability.
Match a request to a nearby, available driver and dispatch the trip.
Track trip lifecycle: requested, matched, en route, in progress, completed.
Compute fare and ETA; apply surge pricing when demand exceeds supply.
nonFunctional: Match within a few seconds even in dense cities.
Ingest millions of driver location updates per second.
Each trip request matches at most one driver; a driver takes one trip at a time.
Fresh location data: 'nearby' must reflect where drivers are now, not minutes ago.
Scale to many cities with wildly uneven density (a few hot cities dominate).
Graceful degradation: widen search or queue rather than fail a request outright.

Location privacy & abuse resistance: driver pings are authenticated and validated (no spoofed/replayed positions reach the index); latest location has a TTL and any history has a bounded retention policy.
Operability: the system exposes the freshness, latency, acceptance, and conflict signals needed to detect degradation per city before it becomes an outage.

capacity.label: Active drivers
capacity.note: Each streams location continuously while online.

capacity.label: Location updates
capacity.note: 10M drivers pinging every ~4s; write-heavy and continuous.

capacity.label: Ride requests
capacity.note: Spiky by city and time (rush hour, events).

capacity.label: Match latency target
capacity.note: Includes nearby query, ranking, and driver acceptance.

capacity.label: Geo query
capacity.note: The core read; must be fast over constantly-moving points.

capacity.label: Density skew
capacity.note: A handful of cities hold most drivers and requests at once.

capacity.label: Hot-city ingest
capacity.note: A top city with ~600k–1M online drivers at ~1 ping/4s. At ~100 bytes/event that's ~15–25 MB/s — sized into ~32–64 stream partitions keyed by geo cell so one cell can't hot-spot a partition.

capacity.label: Geo-query fanout
capacity.note: Each ride request reads ~9 cells and caps to ~50 candidates; downtown cells are split to a finer level so a single cell stays bounded.

capacity.label: Routing QPS
capacity.note: Ranking ~20 candidates by routed ETA per request means ETA calls dominate — hence ETA caching, contraction hierarchies, or a coarse geo pre-filter before precise routing.

capacity.label: Geo-index memory
capacity.note: ~1M drivers x ~1–2 KB (id, cell, product, freshness) per region; cheap to hold in memory, replicated for failover.

api.description: Request a ride. Authenticated rider; Idempotency-Key dedupes retries so a rider has at most one active matching trip. Carries the quote the rider accepted (with its frozen surge multiplier) and the chosen product.

High-frequency driver location update. Fire-and-forget; ingested asynchronously into the geo index. The ping carries authenticated device identity and a monotonic sequence number; the ingest path rejects stale, out-of-order, or replayed pings and runs anti-spoofing checks before the position reaches the index. Latest location is held with a TTL; only bounded history is retained.
api.response: 200 OK (ack) — or 409 if seq is stale / out of order

api.description: Driver accepts a specific offer. The accept names the offer it is answering, so a late retry, a duplicate tap, or an accept of an already-expired offer is rejected rather than racing. First valid acceptance wins: the offer CASes offered->accepted and the driver/trip is claimed atomically; the trip transitions to matched.
api.response: 200 OK { "status": "matched" } — 409 if already taken, or 410 Gone if the offer expired

api.description: Fetch trip status, assigned driver, and live ETA.

dataModel.name: drivers
dataModel.note: Driver profile and current availability. Location is hot and lives in a fast store, not here.

dataModel.name: driver_locations
dataModel.note: Latest location per driver, updated at high frequency. Often a key-value/in-memory store keyed by driver_id; projected into the geo index. Holds only the latest position with a short TTL — a driver whose app stops pinging falls out of 'available' as the entry expires. Retained location history (for safety, support, fraud, or legal/audit) lives in a separate, access-controlled store with a bounded retention policy, never here.

fields.name: updated_at
fields.type: timestamptz (TTL: stale entries expire out of 'available')

dataModel.name: trips
dataModel.note: The trip lifecycle state machine; the unit of matching and billing.

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(matching, matched, en_route, in_progress, completed, cancelled, expired)

fields.name: quote_id
fields.type: uuid (frozen fare + surge multiplier at acceptance)

fields.name: cancel_reason
fields.type: enum null (rider_cancel, driver_cancel, no_drivers, offer_timeout, expired)

dataModel.name: trip_events
dataModel.note: Append-only audit of every state transition (who/what/when), so the trip lifecycle is reconstructable and illegal transitions are detectable after the fact.

dataModel.name: dispatch_offers
dataModel.note: The unit of dispatch: one short-lived offer of a trip to one driver. Accept names the offer it answers, so late/duplicate accepts and accepts of expired offers are rejected. Accept CASes offer offered->accepted and driver available->busy together; fall-through accounting walks attempt_number.

dataModel.name: geo_index
dataModel.note: Spatial index mapping geo cells -> available drivers, enabling fast 'nearby' queries over moving points.

dataModel.name: surge_cells
dataModel.note: Per-cell demand/supply ratio driving the surge multiplier.

patterns.name: Geo-indexing (geohash / S2)
patterns.what: Encode locations into hierarchical cells so 'nearby' becomes a prefix/cell lookup instead of a full scan.
patterns.whenToUse: Proximity queries over many points — ride matching, nearby search, maps.

patterns.name: Write-heavy ingestion pipeline
patterns.what: Absorb a firehose of updates via a stream and async workers rather than synchronous writes.
patterns.whenToUse: Millions of high-frequency updates (driver pings) that can't hit the primary directly.

patterns.name: Matching / assignment
patterns.what: Select the best candidate from a set and assign it atomically so no resource is double-assigned.
patterns.whenToUse: Two-sided marketplaces pairing requests with limited supply.

patterns.name: Distributed lock / CAS
patterns.what: Atomically claim a trip or driver so concurrent offers don't double-book.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple offers race for the same driver or trip.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: Dedupe retried ride requests and accepts so a rider/driver isn't matched twice.
patterns.whenToUse: State-changing calls over an unreliable mobile network.

patterns.name: State machine
patterns.what: Model the trip as explicit states with allowed transitions.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step lifecycles (matching -> matched -> en route -> completed) needing clear invariants.

patterns.name: Geo-sharding by region
patterns.what: Partition the geo index and matching by city/region so a hot city is isolated and queries stay local.
patterns.whenToUse: Extreme density skew where a few cities dominate load.

patterns.name: Real-time demand pricing
patterns.what: Compute a per-cell multiplier from demand/supply to balance the marketplace.
patterns.whenToUse: When supply can't meet demand and you want to ration/incentivize.

steps.title: 1. Request a Ride (the naive baseline)
steps.description: Start simple: a rider posts a request, and the service scans all available drivers to find the nearest one. With a few drivers this is fine; the design exists because there are millions of moving drivers and you can't scan them per request.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you find the nearest driver when there are 10M of them, all moving?

concepts.definition: Pairing a demand-side request (rider) with a supply-side resource (driver).
concepts.whyItMatters: Both sides are dynamic and limited, which shapes indexing and contention.

concepts.definition: Finding points within a radius of a location.
concepts.whyItMatters: Doing it by scanning every driver is O(drivers) per request — hopeless at scale.

talkingPoints: Name the two hard parts up front: ingesting location at scale, and querying nearby fast.
Point out a full scan per request is O(N drivers) — the whole design replaces it.
State the first invariant: a request creates at most one active matching trip per rider (enforced by the idempotency key), so a retry never spawns a second match.

traps.trap: Scanning all drivers and computing distance per request
traps.why: O(drivers) per request collapses under millions of drivers and tens of thousands of requests/sec.
traps.instead: Index drivers spatially so 'nearby' is a cell lookup, not a scan.

bottlenecks.issue: Linear nearest-driver search over a constantly-changing set.
bottlenecks.mitigation: A geospatial index (built up over the next steps).

strong: Identifies the geo-query and ingest scale as the core problems immediately.
Rejects the full scan on complexity grounds.
weak: Treats 'find nearest' as a simple DB query.

whyNow: A naive baseline makes the geo-index and ingest pipeline obvious responses to a concrete bottleneck.

view.caption: The rider app requests through the ride API gateway to the trip service, which scans the entire driver location store and writes the trip to the trips DB.

steps.title: 2. Driver Location Ingestion at Scale
steps.description: Millions of drivers ping location every few seconds. Don't write each ping to the primary synchronously — accept fire-and-forget, push to a stream, and update a fast location store and the geo index asynchronously. Only the latest location per driver matters.
steps.decisionPrompt: 10M drivers ping every 4 seconds. How do you absorb ~2.5M writes/sec without melting your database?

concepts.definition: Only the most recent location for a driver matters; older pings are discarded.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you drop/overwrite instead of storing history, slashing write cost.

concepts.definition: A high-throughput log/queue buffering updates for async processing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples the firehose from the stores so spikes don't overload them.

options.name: Stream + async workers, in-memory latest store (default)
options.description: Pings are accepted fire-and-forget onto a stream, and async workers update an in-memory store and geo index with the latest location per driver.
pros: Absorbs the firehose; workers update stores at their own pace.
In-memory KV (e.g. Redis) keyed by driver_id gives fast last-known location.
cons: Location is eventually consistent (seconds stale).
Stream + workers are extra infrastructure.

view.caption: The gateway feeds pings into a location stream that async workers consume to keep only the latest position in an in-memory driver location store.

options.name: Write each ping to a durable DB
options.description: Every location ping is written synchronously to a durable database, retaining full location history per driver.
pros: Full location history; simple mental model.
cons: ~2.5M durable writes/sec is enormous and mostly wasted (history rarely needed).
The DB becomes the bottleneck.

view.caption: The gateway writes every driver ping straight into a durable driver location store.

traps.why: Millions of writes/sec of data you'll overwrite in 4 seconds wastes the most precious resource (write IOPS).
traps.instead: Keep only the latest per driver in a fast store; stream for async fan-out to the index.

traps.trap: Updating the geo index synchronously in the ping path
traps.why: Index writes on the hot ingest path couple latency and throughput.
traps.instead: Ack the ping immediately; update the index asynchronously off the stream.

deepDives.title: Latest-for-matching vs. retained history, and stale drivers
points: Keep two separate stores: a fast latest-location store (last-write-wins, TTL'd) that feeds matching, and a separate, access-controlled history store for safety, support, fraud, and legal/audit — never matched against the hot path.
Give the latest entry a TTL: a driver whose app stops pinging falls out of 'available' automatically rather than being offered a trip from a minutes-old position.

Validate pings before they update the store — authenticated device identity and a monotonic sequence number drop stale, out-of-order, and replayed pings (covered as a requirement; the auth/risk check sits on the ingest path).

failureDrills.scenario: The ingest stream backs up during a spike.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Location goes a bit staler; the system doesn't drop drivers or crash.
failureDrills.mitigation: Bounded lag with last-write-wins means catching up just means newer pings; matching tolerates seconds of staleness, and a freshness metric/alert flags when a city is matching from stale positions.

strong: Uses last-write-wins to avoid storing history.
Keeps the ping path async and the index off the hot path.
weak: Writes every ping to a durable DB.
Updates the index synchronously per ping.

recap.before: Location was a giant table scanned per request.
recap.after: A firehose of pings is absorbed into a fast latest-location store and the index.
recap.newRisk: Location is eventually consistent — matching must tolerate slight staleness.

whyNow: The geo index is only useful if it's continuously and cheaply fed; ingest comes first.

view.caption: Driver apps ping the gateway every few seconds into the location ingest service, which authenticates and anti-spoof-validates each ping before publishing to a location stream that updates the latest-location store and the geo index cells.

flows.name: Fire-and-forget location ping
flows.note: A ping is accepted immediately and fanned out async to the location store and geo index; only the latest location matters.

steps.title: 3. Geospatial Index for Nearby Drivers
steps.description: Replace the scan with a spatial index. Encode each driver's location into a geo cell (geohash or S2); 'nearby drivers' becomes 'drivers in this cell and its neighbors'. The matching service queries the index, not the raw location store.
steps.decisionPrompt: What cell size do you pick, and how do you avoid missing drivers just across a cell boundary?

concepts.definition: A hierarchical encoding of a region into a string/id where nearby points share prefixes/cells.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns proximity into a cheap prefix/cell lookup instead of distance math over everyone.

concepts.definition: Querying a cell plus its adjacent cells.
concepts.whyItMatters: A driver just over a cell edge is still nearby; you must include neighbors or miss them.

options.name: Geohash buckets in an in-memory store (default)
options.description: Each driver's location is encoded into a fixed geohash cell, so 'nearby' is the same prefix bucket plus its eight neighbors.
pros: Simple prefix math; 'nearby' = same prefix + 8 neighbors.
Cheap to update as drivers move between cells.
cons: Fixed cell sizes don't adapt to density; boundary handling is manual.
Hot cells (downtown) get huge.

view.caption: The matching service queries the geo index by geohash prefix plus neighboring buckets to find nearby drivers.

options.name: S2 cells with adaptive level
options.description: Locations are mapped to hierarchical S2 cells whose level adapts to density, with proper neighbor semantics on a sphere.
pros: Hierarchical levels adapt resolution to density; good neighbor semantics on a sphere.
Battle-tested for planet-scale geo.
cons: More complex library/ops; still needs hot-cell handling.

view.caption: The matching service queries the geo index over an S2 cell range whose level adapts to driver density.

deepDives.title: Cell size and boundaries
points: Cell size trades query precision against result-set size — too big returns far drivers, too small misses them and multiplies neighbor lookups.
Always query the target cell plus adjacent cells so a driver across a boundary isn't missed.
In dense areas a single cell can hold thousands of drivers — cap results and rank, or use a finer level there.

traps.trap: Querying only the rider's exact cell
traps.why: The closest driver may sit one meter across a cell boundary and be missed entirely.
traps.instead: Query the cell plus its neighbors (a ring) and then rank by true distance.

traps.why: Downtown cells overflow while rural cells are empty — both queries degrade.
traps.instead: Use adaptive resolution (S2 levels) or special-case hot cells.

failureDrills.scenario: A downtown cell holds 5,000 available drivers.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Nearby queries stay fast and bounded.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cap candidates per cell, rank by distance/ETA, and consider a finer cell level for dense areas.

strong: Reaches for geohash/S2 and handles cell boundaries with neighbor search.
Recognizes density skew and caps/ranks results.
weak: Invents a custom grid without boundary handling.

recap.before: Finding nearby drivers meant scanning everyone.
recap.after: Nearby is a cheap cell + neighbor lookup over a continuously updated index.
recap.newRisk: Cell sizing and hot cells need tuning; results are as fresh as ingest.

whyNow: The spatial index is the key data structure that makes matching feasible.

view.caption: The location stream maps each driver to a cell in the geo index, which the matching service queries by cell plus neighbors before fetching driver details from the location store.

steps.title: 4. Matching & Dispatch
steps.description: Given nearby candidates, the matching service ranks them (distance/ETA, rating, acceptance likelihood) and dispatches an offer to the best one. The offer is the unit of dispatch: a short-lived record with its own offer_id, expiry, attempt number, and status, delivered via push. Acceptance names the offer it answers, so a late accept, a duplicate tap, or an accept of an already-expired offer is rejected instead of racing. If declined, unanswered, or expired, dispatch falls through to the next candidate. Idempotency stops duplicate requests creating duplicate matches.
steps.decisionPrompt: You found 5 nearby drivers. How do you offer the trip so exactly one ends up assigned?

concepts.definition: Dispatch offers to one (or a few) candidates with a timeout, advancing on decline.
concepts.whyItMatters: Drivers can refuse; matching is a negotiation, not an instant assignment.

concepts.definition: Ordering candidates by ETA, rating, and likelihood to accept.
concepts.whyItMatters: Good ranking cuts time-to-match and improves both sides' experience.

concepts.definition: A short-lived offer of one trip to one driver, with offer_id, expiry, attempt number, and status (offered/accepted/declined/expired).
concepts.whyItMatters: Making the offer a first-class entity is what lets accept prove it answers the current valid offer — late, duplicate, and expired accepts become trivial to reject and fall-through is auditable.

options.name: Sequential best-first offers (default)
options.description: Candidates are ranked and the best one is offered the trip; on decline or timeout the offer moves to the next candidate, one at a time.
pros: A driver is only ever offered one trip at a time — no double-offer confusion.
Simple fairness and clear accounting.
cons: Slower if top candidates decline; each offer costs the timeout.

view.caption: The matching service ranks candidates and the dispatch service offers them to drivers one at a time, falling through to the next on decline.

options.name: Broadcast to several drivers, first to accept wins
options.description: The offer is sent to several nearby drivers at once and the first to accept wins, with the rest released.
pros: Lower time-to-match in sparse areas.
cons: Same driver may get multiple trips' offers at once; needs careful locking to avoid double-assign.
Wasted notifications and driver annoyance.

view.caption: The matching service hands candidates to the dispatch service, which broadcasts the offer to several drivers at once, awarding the trip to the first to accept.

traps.trap: Assigning the trip to a driver without their acceptance
traps.why: Drivers can be unavailable/unwilling; force-assigning strands the rider when the driver no-shows.
traps.instead: Offer with a timeout and confirm on acceptance; advance to the next candidate otherwise.

traps.why: A mobile retry creates a second trip and a second match.
traps.instead: Idempotency key collapses retries to one trip.

traps.trap: Accepting on trip_id + driver_id alone, with no offer identity
traps.why: A driver can accept a trip without proving they're answering the current valid offer — late accepts, duplicate taps, and accepts of an already-expired offer all race.
traps.instead: Make the offer a record with its own id and TTL; accept names the offer_id and CASes it offered->accepted alongside the driver claim.

failureDrills.scenario: The top candidate's app is offline and never answers the offer.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The rider still gets matched promptly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Offer timeout advances to the next ranked candidate; widen the radius if the list is exhausted.

failureDrills.scenario: The push notification carrying an offer is dropped or delayed.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: No driver is silently held; the rider isn't stuck waiting on a lost offer.
failureDrills.mitigation: Offers have a short TTL and require an explicit ack; an unacked offer expires and dispatch falls through to the next candidate, with a backup channel (in-app long-poll/websocket) so delivery doesn't depend on push alone.

strong: Models matching as a timed offer/accept loop with ranking.
Makes requests idempotent and avoids double-offers.

recap.before: We could find nearby drivers but not assign one.
recap.after: Riders are matched via ranked, time-boxed offers to exactly one driver.
recap.newRisk: Concurrent offers can race for the same driver — needs atomic claiming next.

whyNow: A spatial index is only useful once it drives an actual assignment.

view.caption: The gateway routes a request to the matching service, which reads nearby candidates from the geo index, hands the best to the dispatch service for a time-boxed driver offer, and creates the trip.

flows.name: Match -> dispatch offer -> accept
flows.note: The matcher queries nearby candidates, dispatches a time-boxed offer to the best one, and falls through to the next on decline/timeout.

steps.title: 4a. Matching Strategy: Greedy vs Batched
description: Given nearby drivers, how you actually match is a real choice. Greedy matching assigns each rider to the closest available driver the instant they request — simple and low-latency, but locally optimal: it can strand a rider when a slightly-further driver would have been a better global fit.

Batched matching collects requests and drivers over a short window (a few seconds) and solves an assignment problem over the batch, minimizing total wait/ETA — better global outcomes (especially in dense areas) at the cost of a small added delay and more complexity.

Real dispatch leans batched in dense markets (the global gain is worth a 1-2s window) and effectively greedy in sparse ones (no batch to optimize). Either way, the offer is time-boxed and re-offered on decline, and assignment is atomic so two riders never get the same driver.

view.caption: The matching service reads nearby drivers from the geo index and hands a chosen driver to the dispatch service.

options.name: Market-density adaptive policy (default): greedy when sparse, batched when dense
options.description: The matcher chooses by local density: greedy nearest-driver where there's nothing to batch (sparse markets), and short-window batched optimization where the global gain is worth a 1–2s window (dense markets). One policy, two regimes — not a single global default.
pros: Lowest latency to an offer where it matters: sparse markets fall straight through to greedy with no batching delay.
Captures the global-efficiency win of batching exactly where density makes it pay off.
Avoids teaching a single global default — the policy adapts to the market it's running in.
cons: Needs a per-cell density signal and a threshold to switch regimes, plus tuning of the batch window.
Two code paths to operate and test rather than one.
The dense-market path still pays the batch window's added latency and solver cost.

view.caption: The matching service reads density from the geo index cells and picks regime per cell — greedy nearest-driver when sparse, batched optimization when dense — before forwarding to dispatch.

options.name: Batched assignment over a short window
options.description: Requests and drivers are collected over a few seconds and an assignment problem is solved over the batch to minimize total wait/ETA.
pros: Collects requests and drivers over a few seconds and solves an assignment to minimize total wait/ETA.
Better global outcomes in dense markets — the small delay buys a much better overall match.
Can fold in fairness and driver-utilization objectives the greedy path can't.
cons: Adds a batching delay (1-2s) before any offer goes out.
Solving the assignment problem per window is more complex and compute-heavy.
Pointless in sparse markets where each window holds one request — extra latency for no gain.

view.caption: The matching service collects requests from the geo index over a short window into a batch solver that computes an optimal assignment before dispatch.

steps.decisionPrompt: Assign each rider to the nearest driver immediately, or batch requests and optimize? When does each win?

concepts.definition: Greedy = nearest-driver-now (fast, locally optimal); batched = windowed assignment optimization (better global ETA, slight delay).
concepts.whyItMatters: It trades match latency against global efficiency — significant in dense markets.
concepts.example: Batch a few seconds of requests/drivers and solve assignment; fall back to greedy when sparse.

traps.trap: Picking one global strategy — always greedy, or always batched.
traps.why: Always-greedy strands riders and raises wait in dense conditions; always-batched adds pointless latency in sparse markets where each window holds one request.
traps.instead: Adapt by density: batch and optimize where it pays off, fall through to greedy where there's nothing to batch.

recap.after: A density-adaptive matching policy — greedy when sparse, batched when dense.
recap.newRisk: Batching adds latency and needs the atomic-assignment guarantee to avoid double-dispatch.

steps.title: 5. Trip State Machine & Atomic Assignment
steps.description: Model the trip as a state machine (matching -> matched -> en route -> in progress -> completed) and make the matching->matched transition an atomic claim so two riders can't grab the same driver. There are really three coupled states: trip state, driver availability (available/busy), and offer state (offered/accepted/expired) — accept advances all three together. Acceptance is idempotent and first-writer-wins. Cancellation and expiry are first-class transitions: a rider can cancel while matching, a driver can cancel after accept (releasing the driver and re-matching), an unanswered trip expires, and fare is finalized only after completion.
steps.decisionPrompt: Two riders' matches both offer driver D at the same instant and D taps accept on both. Who gets the driver?

concepts.definition: Compare-and-set the driver/trip from available->assigned; only one writer succeeds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents double-booking a driver under concurrent offers.

concepts.definition: Explicit states with allowed transitions and guards.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes invariants (one active trip per driver) enforceable and auditable.

flows.name: Two accepts race for driver D
flows.note: Atomic claim means exactly one wins; the other gets 409.

traps.trap: Marking the trip matched without atomically claiming the driver
traps.why: Two trips can both believe they got driver D — a double-booking.
traps.instead: CAS the driver from available->busy; only the winner proceeds, the loser re-matches.

traps.why: A late 'accept' on an already-cancelled trip corrupts state.
traps.instead: Guard each transition (only matching->matched, etc.); reject illegal ones.

traps.trap: Treating cancellation/expiry as an afterthought outside the state machine
traps.why: A driver who cancels after accept can be left busy, or a cancelled trip's driver never returns to the pool — supply silently leaks.
traps.instead: Model rider-cancel, driver-cancel, and expiry as guarded transitions that release the driver (busy->available) and record a cancel_reason; finalize fare only on completion.

failureDrills.scenario: A driver accepts, then their app crashes before the trip is persisted.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: No driver is silently stuck busy; the trip is recoverable or re-matched.
failureDrills.mitigation: The claim has a TTL; if the trip isn't confirmed, the driver is released and re-offered.

strong: Uses an atomic CAS to claim the driver and guards state transitions.
Handles the crash-after-claim with a TTL release.

recap.before: Concurrent offers could double-book a driver.
recap.after: Each driver and trip transitions atomically through a guarded state machine.
recap.newRisk: Stuck claims need TTL release; the lock store must be fast and available.

whyNow: Matching is only correct once assignment is atomic under concurrency.

view.caption: When a driver accepts via dispatch, the trip service does a compare-and-set in the idempotency store to claim the driver and persists the new state to the trips DB.

options.name: Atomic CAS state transition (default)
options.description: Claiming a driver is a single compare-and-set on the trip's matching->matched transition, so only the first writer wins.
pros: The matching->matched transition is a single compare-and-set, so two riders can't both claim driver D.
Acceptance is idempotent and first-writer-wins; the loser is re-offered the next candidate.
Reuses the trip's own record/state machine — no separate locking system.
cons: Requires a store with an atomic conditional update on the trip/driver row.
Hot drivers in a dense cell can see CAS contention under bursts.
The loser must be detected and re-offered quickly to keep latency low.

view.caption: The trip service uses an atomic compare-and-set in the idempotency store to move the trip from matching to matched, then persists to the trips DB.

options.name: Distributed lock per driver
options.description: A short-lived distributed lock on the driver id is acquired before assignment, guaranteeing exclusive claim and auto-releasing on crash.
pros: Acquire a short-lived lock on the driver id before assigning, guaranteeing exclusive claim.
Conceptually simple mutual exclusion that generalizes beyond a single store.
Lock TTL auto-releases if the holder crashes mid-assignment.
cons: Adds a lock-store dependency on the hot assignment path.
Lock TTL tuning is tricky — too short double-assigns, too long blocks re-offers.
A stale lock from a paused process needs fencing to be fully safe.

view.caption: On accept, the dispatch service grabs a per-driver distributed lock before the trip service writes the trip state to the trips DB.

options.name: Single-writer per driver (serialized queue)
options.description: All offers for a driver are routed to one owning partition that processes claims serially, so ordering decides the winner with no lock.
pros: Route all offers for a driver to one owning partition that processes claims serially — no contention.
First message wins by ordering; no CAS or lock needed within the partition.
Naturally backpressures and avoids races by construction.
cons: Needs consistent partitioning of drivers to owners and rebalancing on failure.
A hot driver's partition can become a throughput bottleneck.
Owner failover must hand off cleanly or claims stall.

view.caption: The dispatch service serializes accepts through a per-driver claim queue where the first claim wins and the trip service persists it.

steps.title: 6. Surge Pricing
steps.description: When demand outstrips supply in an area, raise prices to ration demand and pull in drivers. Compute a per-cell demand/supply ratio over a sliding window into a surge multiplier, applied at quote time. It reuses the same geo cells as matching.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you compute and apply surge per area without making prices flicker every second?

concepts.definition: Open requests vs available drivers in a cell over a window.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the signal that drives the multiplier; smoothing avoids erratic prices.

concepts.definition: Lock the multiplier into the fare estimate shown to the rider.
concepts.whyItMatters: Riders must see a stable price; you can't change it mid-trip.

deepDives.title: Keeping prices stable and fair
points: Compute the ratio over a sliding window and smooth it so prices don't oscillate second to second.
Lock the multiplier into the quote the rider accepts; never change it mid-trip.
Cap surge and be transparent — runaway multipliers erode trust and invite regulation.

Drive surge from validated supply only: a cluster of spoofed or idle drivers shouldn't suppress (or be used to manipulate) the multiplier — reuse the same anti-spoofing signal that gates the geo index.
Keep an audit trail of the demand/supply inputs and multiplier per cell/window; pricing trust and regulation make surge decisions reviewable after the fact.

traps.trap: Recomputing surge per request with no smoothing
traps.why: Prices flicker wildly as a single driver appears/disappears, confusing and angering riders.
traps.instead: Smooth over a window and cache per cell; refresh periodically.

traps.trap: Changing the price after the rider accepted the quote
traps.why: Bait-and-switch destroys trust and may be illegal.
traps.instead: Freeze the multiplier into the accepted quote for the trip.

strong: Computes surge per geo cell over a smoothed window and freezes it at quote time.
Reuses the matching cells and caps the multiplier.
weak: Recomputes per request with no smoothing.

recap.before: One price regardless of local supply/demand.
recap.after: Per-cell surge balances the marketplace, frozen into each quote.
recap.newRisk: Surge math must be smoothed, capped, and transparent to keep trust.

whyNow: With matching working, marketplace balance (pricing) is the next lever in dense, supply-starved areas.

view.caption: The matching service and geo index feed demand and supply signals into the surge/demand store, from which the pricing service reads the cell multiplier to quote the rider.

steps.title: 7. ETA & Routing
steps.description: Riders and matching both need ETAs. A routing service computes travel time over a road graph with live traffic, feeding match ranking (nearest by ETA, not straight-line) and rider-facing arrival estimates. ETAs are cached and refreshed, not recomputed per poll.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why is straight-line distance a bad way to pick the 'nearest' driver, and how do you scale ETA computation?

deepDives.title: Routing is the QPS multiplier — size it
points: If each match ranks ~20 candidate drivers by routed ETA, routing QPS is ~20x ride-request QPS — routing, not matching, is the load that dominates.

Cut it with a coarse geo-distance pre-filter (rank ~20, route only the top few), ETA caching keyed by (origin cell, dest cell), or precomputed contraction hierarchies so each query is a lookup, not a full Dijkstra.
Batched routing (one call over many origin/dest pairs) amortizes graph traversal across a window of requests in a dense cell.

concepts.definition: Shortest travel time over a graph of road segments, weighted by live traffic.
concepts.whyItMatters: A driver 200m away across a river is farther by road than one 500m away on the same street.

concepts.definition: Reusing recent route/ETA results rather than recomputing per request.
concepts.whyItMatters: Routing is expensive; caching and incremental updates keep it affordable at scale.

traps.why: Road layout, one-ways, and rivers make geometric distance a poor proxy for arrival time.
traps.instead: Rank by routed ETA with live traffic; use geo distance only as a cheap pre-filter.

traps.trap: Recomputing the route on every rider poll
traps.why: Routing is costly; per-poll recompute won't scale.
traps.instead: Cache the ETA and update incrementally as the driver moves.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Matching still works, with rougher ETAs.
failureDrills.mitigation: Fall back to geo-distance pre-filter for ranking and show an approximate ETA until routing recovers.

strong: Ranks by routed ETA, using geo distance only as a pre-filter.
weak: Treats straight-line distance as arrival time.

recap.before: Nearness was geometric and ETAs were absent.
recap.after: Matching ranks by real travel time; riders see live ETAs from cached routing.
recap.newRisk: Routing is heavy — it must be cached and have a geo-distance fallback.

whyNow: Good matches and rider trust both depend on real ETAs, not straight-line guesses.

view.caption: The matching service ranks candidates by ETA from the routing service, which routes over the road-graph map data and returns the arrival estimate to the rider.

options.name: Live-traffic routing service (default)
options.description: A routing service computes travel time over a road graph with live traffic, feeding both match ranking and rider-facing ETAs from cached results.
pros: Computes travel time over a road graph with live traffic, so 'nearest' means nearest by ETA, not straight-line.
Feeds both match ranking and rider-facing arrival estimates from one service.
ETAs are cached and refreshed rather than recomputed on every poll.
cons: Maintaining a live road graph with traffic is a heavy, specialized subsystem.
Per-request shortest-path over a large graph is expensive without precomputation.
Traffic data freshness and coverage directly bound accuracy.

view.caption: The routing service computes ETAs over live-traffic road-graph map data for the matching service and returns arrival estimates to the rider.

options.name: Precomputed contraction hierarchies
options.description: The road graph is preprocessed into contraction hierarchies so each ETA query is a fast lookup rather than a full shortest-path search.
pros: Preprocess the road graph into hierarchies so each ETA query is a fast lookup, not a full Dijkstra.
Scales to high query volume — ideal for ranking many candidate drivers per request.
Predictable, low query latency under load.
cons: Live traffic changes require periodically re-running expensive preprocessing.
Heavy upfront build and storage for the contracted graph.
Less responsive to sudden incidents than on-demand routing between rebuilds.

view.caption: The matching service does fast ETA lookups against a contraction-hierarchy index precomputed from the road graph, returning estimates to the rider.

options.name: Haversine straight-line distance
options.description: Candidates are ranked by straight-line great-circle distance between coordinates, with no road graph or routing at all.
pros: Trivial: rank candidates by straight-line distance with no road graph at all.
Effectively free to compute and easy to explain.
A reasonable first-cut filter before a precise ETA pass.
cons: Ignores roads, rivers, and one-ways — the geometrically nearest driver can be far by car.
Picks worse matches, hurting both rider wait and driver efficiency.
No rider-facing arrival estimate worth showing.

view.caption: The matching service ranks candidates by straight-line (haversine) distance from the geo index and gives the rider a rough estimate, with no routing.

steps.title: 8. Scaling by Region (geo-sharding)
steps.description: Density is wildly uneven — a few cities dominate. Shard the geo index and matching by region/city so each operates locally and a hot city is isolated. A region router directs requests and pings to the right shard; cross-region trips (airport runs) are handled explicitly.
steps.decisionPrompt: New Year's Eve makes one city 50x its normal load. How do you keep it from degrading every other city?

concepts.definition: Partitioning the index and matching by geographic region.
concepts.whyItMatters: Most queries are local (driver and rider in the same city), so co-locating them by region keeps queries fast and isolates hot cities.

concepts.definition: A nearby query rarely crosses a city boundary.
concepts.whyItMatters: Region is a natural shard key — it keeps the working set of a query on one shard.

options.name: Region shards with per-region autoscaling (default)
options.description: The geo index and matching are sharded by region/city and a router directs each request to its shard, so a hot city scales in isolation.
pros: A hot city's load is contained; its matching/index scale independently.
Queries stay local — no cross-shard fan-out for the common case.
cons: Cross-region trips (pickup in A, drop in B) need explicit handling.
Region boundaries and rebalancing need tooling.

view.caption: The region router shards traffic to independently autoscaling per-region matching services, isolating a hot city from the rest.

options.name: Single global index for all cities
options.description: One global geo index and matching service handles every city, with no regional sharding or routing.
pros: Simplest; no routing or boundary logic.
cons: One mega-city's spike degrades global query latency.
No blast-radius isolation; the index is a single hot spot.

view.caption: The region router sends all cities to one shared global geo index rather than per-region shards.

deepDives.title: Cross-region trips and boundaries
points: Match in the pickup region; a trip whose dropoff is in another region just updates trip state, not the matching shard.
Riders/drivers near a region boundary may need to query two adjacent region shards.
Promote a city to its own dedicated shard when its load crosses a threshold; rebalance with care.

deepDives.title: Region ownership, handoff, and failover
points: A driver's pings are routed to its current region by the region router (keyed on the driver's geo cell), so the region owning a cell also owns the drivers in it.
When a driver crosses a region boundary, ownership hands off: the new region starts indexing the driver and the old region's stale entry expires via TTL — no driver is double-counted as available.

Region failover rebuilds the geo index from the location stream (it's derived, so it's cheap to reconstruct) while authoritative trip state lives in the durable trips DB — a region can drop and recover without losing trips or stranding drivers as permanently busy.
While a region is degraded, the router can shed it (queue or widen to a neighbor) rather than let its backlog corrupt driver availability elsewhere.

deepDives.title: Degradation tiers (one policy across the system)
points: Ingest lag high (stale-location % climbs): keep matching but prefer fresher pings and surface a freshness alert; never match from minutes-old positions.
No candidates in cell+neighbors: widen the search radius, then relax filters (product), then queue the request briefly, and only then return 'no drivers available'.
Routing brownout: fall back to geo-distance ranking and show an approximate ETA until routing recovers (Step 7).
Push delivery failing: rely on offer TTL + ack and the in-app backup channel; fall through faster (Step 4).
Lock/CAS contention in a hot cell: back off and re-offer; the loser re-matches rather than blocking (Step 5).

Region overload: shed load at the router (queue/widen to neighbor) so one city's spike doesn't degrade the rest. Each tier has a trigger metric, a fallback action, a user-visible behavior, and a recovery condition — all watched by observability.

traps.trap: A single global geo index for the whole planet
traps.why: One mega-event in one city makes every city's nearby query slow; it's a single hot spot.
traps.instead: Shard by region so locality keeps queries local and isolates hot cities.

traps.trap: Sharding by a non-geographic key (e.g. driver_id)
traps.why: Nearby drivers scatter across shards, so every proximity query fans out everywhere.
traps.instead: Shard by region/city so a query's candidates live on one shard.

failureDrills.scenario: A whole region's matching/index goes down mid-evening.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Trips in flight aren't lost; drivers aren't permanently stuck busy; other regions are unaffected.
failureDrills.mitigation: Authoritative trip state is in the durable trips DB; the geo index is derived and rebuilds from the location stream on a standby. The router sheds the region (queue/widen) until it recovers, and driver availability is reconciled from fresh pings rather than stale pre-outage state.

strong: Shards by region for locality and hot-city isolation.
Handles cross-region trips and boundary queries explicitly.
Explains region handoff at boundaries and failover that doesn't corrupt driver availability.
weak: Shards by a key that scatters nearby drivers.

recap.before: One global index made every city share fate.
recap.after: Per-region shards keep queries local and isolate hot cities.
recap.newRisk: Cross-region trips and boundary queries need explicit handling; routing/rebalancing is ongoing work.

whyNow: At global scale with extreme density skew, a shared index is the final bottleneck and blast-radius risk.

view.caption: The gateway routes requests by region through the region router to per-region matching services, each backed by its own regional geo index.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: drivers stream location through an authenticated, anti-spoof-validated async ingest pipeline into a per-region geo index; a region router directs ride requests to the right regional matching service, which queries nearby candidates, ranks them by routed ETA, and dispatches time-boxed offers (delivered via push with ack/expiry); the trip is an atomically-claimed state machine; surge pricing balances each cell; ETA/routing feeds both ranking and rider estimates; observability tracks freshness, latency, acceptance, and conflict signals per region so degradation is caught before it becomes an outage.

view.caption: Driver pings flow through ingest and an auth/risk check into the location stream, latest store, and regional geo indexes; rider requests are region-routed to matching, which uses ETA, pricing, and dispatch (via push) to assign drivers and persist atomic trip state; observability watches lag, offer/accept, and conflict rates.

functional.how: Idempotent POST /rides creates a trip and triggers matching.
Async ingest pipeline absorbs the ping firehose with last-write-wins.

functional.requirement: Match and dispatch a nearby driver
functional.how: Geo-index nearby query feeds ranked, time-boxed offers.
An explicit, atomically-claimed trip state machine.
Routing service for ETA; per-cell surge multiplier frozen at quote.

nonFunctional.how: Geo-index cell lookups plus ETA-ranked offers keep time-to-match low.
Stream + async workers with last-write-wins, off the primary.
Atomic CAS claim on the driver/trip transition.
Continuous index updates; matching tolerates seconds of staleness.
Geo-sharding by region isolates hot cities and keeps queries local.

A single degradation policy with explicit tiers: widen radius / advance candidates on decline, geo-distance fallback when routing degrades, offer TTL + backup channel when push fails, back-off + re-offer under lock contention, and router load-shedding under regional overload.

Authenticated, sequence-numbered pings validated by an auth/risk check before they reach the index; TTL on latest location and bounded retention for any history, kept separate from the matching hot path.

Observability tracks location-stream lag, geo-query latency, offer timeout/acceptance rate, CAS conflict rate, hot-cell cardinality, and per-region error-budget burn, tied to user-visible SLOs (time-to-match, location freshness, ETA error).

say: Restate it: match a rider to a nearby available driver in seconds, with millions of drivers moving.
List functional (request, locate, match, trip lifecycle, pricing) and non-functional (latency, ingest scale, no double-book).
Name the two hard problems: ingesting location at scale and querying 'nearby' fast.
Size drivers, ping rate (=> updates/sec), and request rate; note the write-heavy location firehose.
Sketch POST /rides (idempotent), the high-frequency location update, and accept.
Start with the naive scan to expose the geo-query problem, then add ingest, then the geo index.
Add matching/dispatch, the atomic trip state machine, then surge and ETA.
Go deep where pushed: geohash vs S2 and cell boundaries, or atomic assignment.
Geo-shard by region to isolate hot cities; handle cross-region trips.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the dominant risk (hot-cell / hot-city density).

expectations: Recognizes that scanning all drivers won't scale and reaches for a spatial index.
Separates high-frequency location data from the trip database.
Designs geohash/S2 indexing with neighbor (boundary) queries and an async ingest pipeline.
Models matching as ranked time-boxed offers with an atomic trip claim.
Ranks by routed ETA, not straight-line distance.
Geo-shards by region for locality and hot-city isolation; handles cross-region and boundaries.
Reasons about staleness vs. freshness in the location pipeline and its effect on matching.
Designs surge to be smoothed, capped, and frozen at quote; quantifies the tradeoffs.

followUps: How would you support carpooling/pooled rides (matching multiple riders to one driver en route)?
How do you prevent and detect GPS spoofing by drivers gaming incentives or surge?
How would you handle scheduled/reserved rides hours in advance alongside on-demand?
How do you keep the rider's live map of the approaching driver smooth and accurate?
How would you match for airport queues or venues where FIFO driver fairness matters?
How do you reconcile the fast location store, the geo index, and trip state after a regional outage?
